title: Desperate House Sellers: Distress among Developers
abstract: I identify the effect of financial constraints on product prices using granular data on home-builder housing developments from the 2006–2009 housing crisis. Builders who experience losses in one area subsequently sell homes in unaffected areas at a discount to raise cash quickly. When builders cut prices, they sell homes faster and builders cut prices more in areas in which price cuts produce larger declines in time-to-sale. Financially constrained firms are more likely to cut prices of homes in healthy areas following losses elsewhere. Firms cut prices following losses in other projects only during the crisis, not during the boom.

article.1 Home Building Overview:
JEL E30 - General G01 - Financial Crises G30 - General G31 - Capital Budgeting; Fixed Investment and Inventory Studies; Capacity R30 - General Editor: Manju Puri Manju Puri Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Financial frictions can negatively affect economic activity. When firms are unable to access external financing, critical firm decisions may be distorted. Firms may reduce investment in positive net present value (NPV) projects when financing constraints bind, affecting employment (Chodorow-Reich 2014;Giroud and Mueller 2019), investment (Lamont 1997), and innovation (Howell 2017;Krieger, Li, and Papanikolaou 2022). But evidence of financial constraints influencing prices—a crucial firm decision affecting inflation, product competition, and consumer welfare—is limited.1I present new findings on how financial constraints affect product pricing using granular data on the geographically distinct housing developments of home builders. I show that during the housing crisis, when builders lost money on one project, they cut prices on homes they had for sale in healthy, geographically distant projects in order to speed up sales and generate cash quickly.

Empirical concerns make studying the effect of financial constraints on prices difficult. One of these concerns involves measurement. Precise data on product prices, sales, and firm financial constraints is often unavailable. A second concern is endogeneity. Firms facing financial constraints may also experience economic shocks that separately affect prices. Financially constrained firms may cut prices because they need to boost sales and raise cash quickly, or because they operate in areas facing large drops in demand for their product.

I overcome these empirical challenges using the home builder industry as a natural laboratory. Using home sales data, I construct a novel micro data set recording product price, quality, and time-to-sale along with measures of firm finances. To estimate the effect of financial constraints on prices, I compare pricing between builders who differ in their precrisis exposure to housing bust areas. Builders with greater precrisis exposure to bust areas experienced greater losses during the crisis, and these losses tightened their financial constraints. A builder’s losses in bust areas are plausibly exogenous to other variables that drive pricing, for two reasons. First, builder projects are geographically distinct: I measure price effects in regions separate from where builders initially lost money. Second, project locations are chosen before the housing collapse and the housing collapse in bust regions was largely unanticipated.

I find that home builders facing financial constraints cut product prices to make sales and generate cash quickly. A one standard deviation decrease (approximately 10%) in the value of a builder’s projects in other markets leads to a 2.2% decrease in his house prices in unaffected areas. I show that when builders cut prices, they sell homes more quickly and that builders spread price shocks more to areas where a price cut produces a larger decline in time-to-sale.

I perform several cross-sectional tests to probe the financial constraints mechanism. First, the effect on prices is stronger for firms with higher measures of financial constraints, for example, higher leverage ratios and lower coverage ratios before the housing crisis. Second, I find no effect on prices during the housing boom, a period when financing constraints did not bind because builders could easily obtain credit. I also show that the effect of financial constraints on prices is increasing in the builder inventory to sales ratio. This result is consistent with a fire sale mechanism in which constrained firms become more likely to cut prices when they have more inventory available to liquidate (Kim 2021). Finally, I show that builders are less likely to cut prices on lower-quality homes, which are more sensitive to the builder’s reputation, suggesting that concerns about reputation may mitigate the effect of financial constraints on prices.

To analyze the home builder industry, I construct a unique data set of builder home sales matched to builder finances. I start with a CoreLogic data set of housing transactions that records rich information on the characteristics of homes, such as square footage and property type. I use these variables to control for differences in home quality between builders that would otherwise confound the results. Next, I construct a variety of financial constraints measures using builder annual reports, some that are generic in the corporate finance literature and some that are unique to the home building industry, and merge these to builder home sales. Finally, to investigate if constrained builders cut prices to speed up sales, I gather data from realtor listings that report homes’ time on the market and merge these listings to home sales. The resultant data set reports a home’s location, price, quality, and time-to-sale, creating a novel setting to study financial constraints and prices.

To conduct my empirical analysis, I compare prices for observationally equivalent homes sold in the same ZIP code and year by different builders who differ in their exposure to house price shocks in other areas. I measure a builder’s exposure to price shocks as the weighted average change in prices in counties where the builder operates, with weights that reflect the importance of each county in the builder’s overall portfolio. If I were to evaluate the effect of a builder losing money in a given county on the builder’s pricing in that same county, then any effect I find would be contaminated by local economic conditions. In contrast, in my empirical design I separate local economic conditions from the builder’s condition by evaluating a constrained builder’s pricing in a region separate from the one in which the builder initially lost money.

The validity of my analysis rests on the assumption that a builder’s initial decision to build in a housing bust area is independent of his potential pricing. This assumption will be violated if the house price shock affects all regions and the builders who located in bust areas serve a clientele more negatively affected by the common shock. To address this concern, I perform a number of tests. First, if builders with exposure to bust markets (shocked), cater to a different clientele than builders without that exposure (nonshocked), then I would expect these builders to also differ in pricing before the crisis. To test this, I show graphically that pricing for shocked and nonshocked builders only diverges during the crisis period and not before. Second, if shocked builders sold to a different socioeconomic group, then there should be a difference in the luxury, quality, and neighborhood of the homes these builders sell. To address this possibility, I show the effect remains robust to controlling for ZIP code by year fixed effects and a host of variables describing home quality.

This paper contributes to the literature on credit constraints and prices; papers in this area have come to differing conclusions. One strand of the literature holds that if customer bases are sticky and price discounts are an investment in building future market share, then financially constrained firms may raise prices to boost profits in the short-term and forgo attracting new customers (Chevalier and Scharfstein 1996). Consistent with this argument,Chevalier (1995)shows that in the supermarket industry, heavily leveraged firms raise prices of inventory in order to access cash flow more quickly.Gilchrist et al. (2017)also finds that liquidity constrained firms raise prices, studying goods underlying the PPI. A second, alternative, literature instead emphasizes that distressed firms, lacking a sticky customer base and unable to access external finance, sell output at a discount to raise cash quickly (Bernanke and Gertler 1989;Kiyotaki and Moore 1997;Shleifer and Vishny 1992;Shleifer and Vishny 2011). Consistent with this theory,Pulvino (1998)finds evidence in the airline industry that distressed firms sell aircraft at a discount to industry outsiders. More recently,Kim (2021)finds in the consumer goods industry that credit constrained firms cut prices on inventory to boost cash flow. My results lend support to the fire sale inventory mechanism inKim (2021)andPulvino (1998). Using novel micro-data on product quality, price, and time-to-sale, I show in a durable goods industry that distressed firms discount prices on inventory in order to make sales quickly.

My results also contribute to a broad literature in corporate finance on internal capital markets. Papers in this area show that internal capital markets affect investment (Lamont 1997),2employment (Giroud and Mueller 2019), and lending (Peek and Rosengren 2000).Ge (2017)also studies how internal capital markets affect prices but focuses specifically on the life insurance industry, showing that distressed insurers lower premiums on contracts that increase firm capital. Little is known about the effect of internal capital markets on prices in durable goods industries, where buyers and sellers face search frictions and sellers must invest time to find a buyer with a high value for their good. I show in the home builder industry that firms respond to a loss in one project by slashing prices in geographically distant projects, and I use detailed time-to-sale data from listings records to show that firms cut prices to speed up sales. My findings have implications for price contagion in other durable goods industries, such as the automobile and household goods industries.

In addition, my findings contribute to a growing literature on the propagation of local geographic shocks (Bailey et al. 2018;Benmelech et al. 2019;Campbell, Giglio, and Pathak 2011;DeFusco et al. 2018;Notowidigdo 2020).Giroud and Mueller (2019)show that when a negative economic shock affects one branch of a firm, the firm cuts employment in other, geographically distant branches. I also show that firms spread negative economic shocks across geography via their internal networks, but my paper has several important differences from theirs. First, whereasGiroud and Mueller (2019)study employment, I study pricing, a new margin of geographic contagion. Second,Giroud and Mueller (2019)do not observe data on firm clientele, leaving them less able to address the concern that a firm cuts employment in an unaffected project because the firm’s clientele has reduced demand. In contrast, I have granular data on the physical characteristics of homes that builders sell, and I use these data to capture clientele differences between builders that would otherwise confound the results.

Lastly, my findings add to the literature on home builders and the housing crisis.Haughwout et al. (2012)andNathanson and Zwick (2018)show that during the housing boom, home builders overdeveloped and created an excess supply of homes, fueling the bust in house prices. My results provide evidence that home builders further contributed to the housing crisis by cutting home prices in unaffected regions.
1.1 Home building business landscape

The home building industry is an important part of the U.S. economy. In 2017, 13% of homes sales were of newly constructed homes, with a total market size of roughly $236 billion. During the last housing boom the industry was even larger; in 2005, at a market size of approximately $381 billion, the home building industry was responsible for 20% of home sales.3However, despite its considerable size, the industry is also fragmented, with a few large firms and many small builders. In 2007, the U.S. Economic Census reported a total of 98,067 home builders. These builders range from large, publicly traded firms producing thousands of homes a year, to one-person contractors hired by a home buyer to build a home to their specification. Indeed, in 2007, 65% of home builders had sales receipts of less than $1 million.

The large home builders have wide geographic dispersion.Table 1reports summary statistics on the geographic dispersion of the builders in my sample. My sample, which is described in detail in Section 2, contains roughly 2,000 large builders. The average builder sells 72 homes per year and operates in approximately 3 different states. The average builder in my sample has an HHI of 0.51, suggesting equal concentration across two states.4Public home builders are larger and more dispersed than private ones; the average public home builder sells 4,265 homes per year and operates in 14 states.

1.2 Home building financing

This paper focuses on large builders, defined as selling more than two homes per year and operating in more than one county. I focus my analysis of builder finances on the subset of large builders that is publicly traded, and therefore, reports financial information. Public builders finance themselves using cash from operations, unsecured public debt, revolving credit facilities, mortgage notes, and operating leases.Table 1reports summary statistics on builder financing taken from the annual reports of public builders.

From the 1980s until 2007, land prices did not fall in a significant way.5Going into the crisis, builders carried large amounts of land on their balance sheet, not anticipating large drops in prices.6During the crisis, land prices suffered unprecedented declines, by as much as 40%–60%, with widespread dispersion across metros, wiping out builders’ asset values.7Otherwise healthy builders suddenly came close to insolvency. By 2009, 14% of public home builders operating in 2006 had been acquired or gone bankrupt. Falling asset values effectively cut off many builders from external financing during the crisis. Builders were forced to rely on internally generated funds. This led them to sell off assets at a discount.8

Figure 1illustrates how declining land values contributed to a deteriorating financial condition for builders. The upper-left panel plots land under development over time. Land under development peaks in 2006 and falls by more than half in 2008. This decline should reflect three factors: builders selling off land, land value impairments, and builders choosing to postpone development of their holdings. Both lots owned and lots optioned (shown in the upper-right panel) peaked in 2005, before falling sharply, and thus follow a boom-bust pattern similar to land under development. Lots optioned rose more quickly in the boom and also fell more quickly in the bust than lots owned outright. Since options are easier to dispense with than land, this suggests builders may have preferred to sell off more land during the crisis than they actually did.

The decline in land values led to a dramatic increase in the leverage ratio of builders from 2005 to 2009, shown in the bottom-left panel ofFigure 1. The increase in leverage is all the more striking because builders stopped issuing debt during the crisis, asInternet Appendix Figure A.2illustrates. The overall leverage increase implies that the decline in builder asset values overwhelmed the decline in debt issuance. Lastly, the bottom-right panel ofFigure 1plots the average builder coverage ratio over time, defined as the ratio ofEBITto interest expense. The coverage ratio proxies for a firm’s ability to service its debt out of cash flow. The average coverage ratio becomes negative in 2008, reflecting the fact that the average builder was making a loss during the crisis.

Builder A loses money on homes in county 1, and subsequently cuts prices in county 3, relative to builder B’s prices, in order to make sales more quickly. Open in new tab Download slide

Together, these figures suggest that the decline in land prices damaged builders’ finances, effectively cutting builders off from external financing. Builders who experienced large losses on projects in housing bust areas would have been severely financially constrained, unable to borrow externally.
article.2 Data and Summary Statistics:
For my analysis I merge home sales transactions to home builders’ finances and home listing records. This section discusses the merger of the data and the construction of the variables used in the paper.

My main source of data is the tax and deed databases from CoreLogic. For details on this data set, seeInternet Appendix Section B. I merge the home purchase information from the deed database to the house characteristics’ information in the tax database to create a data set of housing transactions with physical characteristics of homes.9I impose a number of filters on the data in order to create a sample representative of the U.S. new home market.10To validate my identification of new home sales, I compare my data to new housing permits from the Census inInternet Appendix Figure C.3. Next, I clean the seller name field using various string matching algorithms. String cleaning methods are not sufficient to completely group homes to builders however, because several large builders sell homes under subsidiary name brands. To ensure that I group together homes sold by the same company but under different brand names, I manually match subsidiary builder names to parent builder names for the public builders and largest private builders. I validate my assignment of homes to builders using industry data inInternet Appendix Figure C.4.

To focus on home builders of reasonable size, I remove builders who sell no more than two homes per year. To focus on builders with sufficient geographic dispersion, I require that builders sell homes in more than one county. These restrictions narrow the sample to 98,151 home sales, corresponding to approximately 25% of new homes sales made in the United States in 2009. These home sales are made by a total of 2,054 unique builders.

Table 2reports summary statistics of the homes sold in this sample. The rich housing characteristics that CoreLogic provides, such as number of bathrooms and square footage, go a long way toward determining the price of a home and therefore are very useful controls for home quality. The average home in my sample sells for $263,228 in 2009 dollars; for the subset of homes sold by public builders, the average home sells for $260,942. 92% of homes in the sample are single family as opposed to condominiums, reflecting the fact that home builders tend to sell standalone homes in subdivisions. Large public builders often have affiliated financing arms that offer mortgages to their customers. In 2009, builder mortgage arms financed 32% of home sales in my sample.11CoreLogic reports additional controls, such as fireplace type, roof type, and condition type, but these variables are not populated for a large fraction of homes. Including all variables makes the sample size drop significantly, so I do not use them in the main specification. Nonetheless, I show in robustness checks that the results remain similar when these variables are included.

I use the updated seller name field to merge builder homes to builder financial information from Compustat, DealScan, and SDC Platinum. I obtain builder financial information from Compustat as well as variables specific to home builders, such as lots owned, lots owned under option, land under development, and equity in unconsolidated subsidiaries. I am able to obtain financial information from Compustat for 33 home builders over the period 2000–2015. However, in 2009, only 13 builders report finances in Compustat. I use DealScan to obtain syndicated bank loan issuances for both public and large private home builders. I use SDC Platinum to obtain information on public bond issuances of builders. Lastly, I use the Zillow House Price Index for county-level house prices.

For time-to-sale information, I use CoreLogic’s Multiple Listing Service (MLS) data set, which has detailed records on home listings as reported by real estate agencies. I merge the data set of builder home sales in 2009 to the MLS data set using the APN identifier and county code of the home. For further details, seeInternet Appendix Section B.2. Panel B ofTable 2reports summary statistics on builder listings. Each observation represents a builder home sale matched to listings record. Approximately one-quarter of builder home sales in the main estimation sample can be matched to a listing record. Builders sell homes through their own offices within a development and advertise homes by listings on their websites, often not using realtors. As a result, realtors often miss builder listings in MLS, leading to the relatively low match rate between builder homes and listings. The average builder home sells approximately 6 months after its original listing, and homes sold by public builders sell slightly faster. Prices and home characteristics within the sample of homes that have a listing record are similar to the prices and characteristics of homes in the larger estimation sample described in panel A ofTable 2.
article.3 Identification Strategy:
An ideal experiment to test the effect of financial constraints on prices would randomly assign a subset of firms to lose access to external financing. Finding that the financially constrained firms cut prices, while the unconstrained firms make no adjustment, is evidence of financial constraints at work. Approximating this experiment in the real world is difficult due to data limitations and endogeneity concerns. First, firms do not report financial constraints, making it challenging to identify affected firms. Second, finding a financing shock that randomly affects firms is difficult. When comparing two firms, one facing a financial constraints shock and one not, it is possible that the company with the negative shock is mismanaged compared to the unshocked company. If so, mismanagement would explain both why banks no longer lend to the firm and why the firm’s prices fall, essentially demand for the firm’s products drops.

Studying financial constraints in the home builder industry using a geography-based identification addresses these concerns.Figure 2outlines a stylized example of my research design. In this example, I compare two companies, A and B, selling homes in three counties, 1, 2, and 3. Builder A builds homes in counties 1 and 3, and builder B builds homes in counties 2 and 3. If house prices drop significantly in county 1, where builder A has homes for sale but builder B does not, then builder A will experience a negative profit shock. If builder A is constrained by its losses in county 1, it may need to raise cash quickly and therefore may choose to sell its homes in the healthy county 3 quickly and therefore at a discount. By comparing house prices in county 3, where both A and B sell homes but which has no house price shock, I can identify whether or not builder A cut prices in response to a loss in another project.

I measure profit loss as a builder’s exposure to house price changes in other counties. I am able to use local house prices to approximate project profits thanks to characteristics of home building inventory. To illustrate this, consider an industry in which prices do not proxy for profits, such as pencil manufacturers operating in multiple regions. A shock to the price of pencils in one region would not necessarily reduce the profits of the pencil manufacturer, because the firm can scale down production of pencils in the shocked region and transport existing pencils to areas with higher prices. In contrast, home building inventory is immobile and requires high upfront investment. Builders also have long operating cycles: it takes between 3 to 5 years to purchase a parcel of land, develop it, and build and sell a home. As a result, if local house prices fall steeply below what was expected when the homes were first developed, builders will be unable to recoup costs on production or to transfer inventory to areas with higher demand, causing their profits to fall. These unique features of home building inventory allow me to capture a project’s profits using local house prices.12

A central threat to this strategy is the possibility that builders serve different clienteles and that these clienteles respond differently to a common regional house price shock. By themselves, common regional housing shocks do not threaten the design because I compare builder home sales to other builder home sales in the same area. If there were common regional shocks, then all builders in an area would be shocked together and I should find no effect. Indeed, common regional house price shocks only pose a problem insofar as builders also serve different economic groups, and these groups respond differently to the common regional shock. As an example, suppose that builder A builds homes for blue collar workers whereas builder B builds homes for white collar workers. Additionally, suppose a common regional shock occurs and white collar workers are much less affected than blue collar workers. Then the prices of builder A’s homes may fall relative to builder B’s, because builder A’s clients, responding to the common shock, decrease their demand for homes, whereas builder B’s clients are unaffected and their demand remains constant.

To address this problem, I exploit the rich geographic and structural detail in CoreLogic. I restrict to home sales made in 2009 and use ZIP code fixed effects to compare homes sold within the same ZIP code and year. I also control for observables of the home, such as square footage and bathrooms.13It is unlikely that structurally similar homes, on the market in the same ZIP code and in the same year, sell to different socioeconomic clienteles. I also address this problem by testing a specification which makes it less likely that a builder’s shock is common to all regions. I calculate a builder’s shock using only geographically distant counties and show that the results remain similar using this modified shock measure.

Of course, the decision of where to initially build developments is not randomly assigned. Builders choose locations based on proximity to headquarters, projections for income and population growth, and local land use regulations. The validity of my analysis rests on the assumption that builders’ decisions to build in certain states during the housing boom period do not correlate with their potential pricing in 2009, conditional on physical and geographic characteristics of homes. I test the validity of this assumption by showing that exposed and unexposed builders’ pricing follow parallel trends before the crisis.
article.4 Empirical Analysis and Results:
4.1 Empirical specification

My empirical strategy analyzes the effect of builder exposure to shocked regions on the prices of builder homes in unshocked regions. I begin by constructing a measure of a builder’s exposure to house price changes in distant counties in 2009.14Suppose a builderjoperates in counties1,…,Kin 2006. The shock to builderj, in monthtin 2009, in countyk, is defined as the weighted average of the change in house prices between 2006 and 2009 in all other counties1,…,Kthe builder has homes in, excluding countyk. The change in house prices in a county is calculated using Zillow. The weights are the fraction of the number of homes builderjhas sold in 2006 in a given countyr, over the total number of homes builderjsold in 2006.

I include controlsHPt,kfor logarithm of the county-level house prices each month.18All specifications also include ZIP code fixed effects,γγs(i)⁠. Since I evaluate the effect only in 2009, ZIP code fixed effects are equivalent to ZIP code by year fixed effects. This specification allows me to compare sales of homes within a narrow geography and small time frame. ControlsXiinclude variables describing the characteristics of the housei, including: number of bathrooms, square footage, and property type (i.e., single family home, condo, or duplex). The geography fixed effects and the vector of structural controls go a long way toward fully describing the price of the home. Standard errors are clustered at the county level throughout. In robustness exercises inInternet Appendix Section C.6I cluster at the builder level and find that the results are similar.19

Before reviewing the results, I summarize the threats to validity for the design. First, it is possible that the shock metric I define may not completely capture builder exposure to ailing markets. I define a builder’s exposure to a region according to the number of homes a builder sells there in 2006. However, if homes sold does not proxy well for inventory, then I could mismeasure the shock. For example, if a builder has many homes on the market in Las Vegas during the housing bust, he may in fact sell very few of those homes, because demand has dried up. This builder’s asset values would have indeed dropped, but the builder’s shock would not fall because the shock is calculated off of homes sold, not off of unsold inventory. This will introduce observations into the analysis which have an incorrectly small shock value and a large price response, which should bias the result downward.

A related problem is the possibility that multiple county builders respond differently to common regional shocks than single county builders. A single county builder has zero exposure to other counties, and therefore will always have a shock of zero. During the crisis, large multiple county builders, having at least some exposure to bust states, would often have negative shocks. As a result, the shock could correlate with builder dispersion and hence with builder size. If large builders happen to respond differently to common shocks than small builders, then any effect found could be due to builder size rather than to financial constraints. To address this concern, I restrict the sample to homes sold by large, multiple county builders.

Another potential threat is the possibility that builder geographic networks overlap with networks of other agents, such as lenders, who may also spread shocks across geography (Mondragon 2018;Gilje, Loutskina, and Strahan 2016;Cortés and Strahan 2017). I address this threat in my design because I compare homes selling within the same ZIP code and year. If a lender experiences losses in a housing bust area and transmits those losses to healthy areas by cutting lending, then shocked and nonshocked builders in the healthy area should be equally affected; a credit shock common to an entire ZIP code cannot explain differential pricing between builders within the same ZIP code.

These results indicate that builders cut inventory prices in response to financial constraints. Earlier work byChevalier (1995)shows an opposite result to mine: in the supermarket industry, constrained firms raise prices of inventory in order to access cash flow more quickly. My result differs from hers because the nature of supermarkets differs from that of home building. The two industries have different methods of accessing cash flow quickly. A supermarket generates cash in the short run by raising prices on its goods. This is costly in the long run because higher prices today lead to customers over time switching to competitors, resulting in a lower market share in the future. In contrast, home builders have a weaker incentive to grow market share by cutting prices, because home builders compete with large numbers of resale homes. Instead, a home builder cuts prices on homes in order to sell them more quickly and thereby generate cash quickly. My results suggest that the effect found byChevalier (1995)may change depending on the degree of concentration in an industry.22I explore how the effect of financial constraints on prices changes with concentration and reputational concerns in Section 5.5.

The granular housing controls inEquation (2)make it unlikely that shocked builders build homes of lower quality than unshocked builders, which would explain the result in place of a financial constraints mechanism. However, some unobserved difference in the quality of homes between shocked and unshocked builders still may not be picked up by the controls. If so, then a builder’s shock should also predict prices before the crisis. To test this possibility, I run a placebo test in which I evaluate the effect of the shock in all years between 2000 and 2015, not just in 2009 as inEquation (2).Equation (3)defines this placebo specification: now the sample includes first sales of all new homes sold between 2000 and 2015.

article.5 Support for Financial Constraints Mech:
5.1 Predictions

I have shown that when firms experience losses on distant projects, they cut prices on homes in unaffected areas. I hypothesize that these discounted prices in unaffected areas are a response to financial constraints: when firms cannot access external financing, they sell inventory at a discount to access cash flow quickly. This is consistent with models of “socialist” firms that cross-subsidize projects when financing constraints bind (Stein 1997;Scharfstein and Stein 2000;Ge 2017). This mechanism yields a set of predictions that I will detail below and test in the rest of the section.

First, I expect that the effect of distant losses on prices should increase with the level of preexisting financial constraints. The more that preexisting financial constraints bind, the more that losses from one project affect another project’s pricing (Giroud and Mueller 2019).

Second, I expect that in periods when the firm is not financially constrained—either because of abundant internal funds or because of easy access to external finance—then prices will not be linked across projects (Giroud and Mueller 2019). It follows that during the housing boom, when builders had both abundant internal cash and easy access to credit, prices should not be linked across projects. Builders should also have no reason to adjust prices following a positive shock, unless prices were already set at liquidation levels due to preexisting financial constraints.

A final set of predictions relates to firm listing times. Builder assets consist largely of illiquid inventories of land and homes. If builders cut prices because they are constrained and trying to raise cash quickly, then when constrained builders cut prices they should also sell homes faster. Relatedly, I expect a stronger effect on prices for firms with greater levels of inventory available to liquidate, consistent with a fire sale mechanism (Kim 2021).24Finally, firms should be more likely to cut prices in areas where a price cut produces a larger decline in time-to-sale.

5.2 Financial constraints heterogeneity

In this section I test first, if the tendency to cut prices is stronger for firms that were more financially constrained on the eve of the housing crisis, and second, if the effect of builder losses on prices appears only during periods of costly external finance.

To test whether builders are more likely to cut prices in response to distant losses if they had been more constrained in the pre-period, in 2006, I estimate the following specification:

If the tendency to cut prices following losses in other projects is always stronger for firms with higher measures of financial constraint in the pre-period, then it becomes more likely that the effect is driven by financial constraints.

The results inTable 4show a strong positive relationship between a builder’s financial position on the eve of the housing crisis and his tendency to cut prices in 2009 in response to sudden financial distress. The coefficient for the interaction of the shock and pre-period leverage, reported in the second column of panel A, shows that the effect of the shock increases by 1.31 percentage points when moving from the bottom of the pre-period leverage distribution to the top (25th to 75th percentile).

The third column of panel A reports the coefficient for the interaction between the shock measure and pre-period builder coverage. The coverage ratio is defined as the ratio ofEBITto interest expense; this measure captures a firm’s ability to pay its debt out of cash flow. The coefficient for the interaction term is negative, and shows that the effect of the shock increases by 0.35 percentage points when moving from the top of the pre-period coverage distribution to the bottom (75th to 25th percentile). Unlike with the leverage ratio, I move from the top to the bottom of the distribution because higher coverage indicates greater financial well-being. Firms with worse financial metrics on the eve of the crisis were more likely to respond to distress in 2009 by cutting prices.

Next, I use institutional details of the home building industry to construct measures of constraints unique to builders. The financial constraints proxies in column 4 of panel A and columns 1 and 2 of panel B refer to these industry-specific constraints. Equity in subsidiaries as a fraction of assets captures the extent to which a builder invested in joint ventures in 2006. Builders will occasionally develop land with a partner, such as another builder or investment fund, when the land lot is large and carries more risk. Having more equity in these joint ventures or subsidiaries suggests a builder is more hedged to large swings in real estate values. The coefficient for the interaction term of equity in subsidiaries as a fraction of assets is negative, and shows that the effect of the shock increases by 0.38 percentage points when moving from the top of the distribution to the bottom (75th to 25th percentile). The results are similar when looking at equity in subsidiaries using levels (column 1 of panel B) rather than as a fraction of assets. Firms with more undiversified exposure to land values going into the crisis were more likely to cut prices following losses in other projects.

Column 2 of panel B reports the coefficient for the interaction between the shock measure and the ratio of land a builder owned under option to land owned outright in 2006. When land values plummeted in 2009, builders with more of their land owned via options rather than owned outright were in a stronger financial position. The coefficient for the interaction of the shock and the option ratio is negative, and shows that the effect of the shock increases by 0.86 percentage points when moving from the top of the pre-period option ratio distribution to the bottom (75th to 25th percentile). This result indicates that firms with less flexibility over their land holdings were more prone to spread shocks across projects in 2009.

The results in columns 2–4 of panel A and columns 1 and 2 of panel B ofTable 4investigate how the tendency to cut prices following losses in other projects varies with measures of preexisting financial constraint for the subset of firms reporting financial information.26In column 3 of panel B, I test whether the tendency to cut prices varies along a measure of financial constraint that can be observed for all builders.Kim (2021)argues that if constrained firms cut prices to generate cash quickly, then the tendency to cut prices should increase with the level of the firm’s illiquid inventory. Having more inventory to liquidate implies the firm should be more likely to cut prices when constrained. I adapt this idea to the builder setting and test whether builders with higher inventory to sales ratios in 2006 are more likely to cut prices following distress in 2009. I do not observe inventory or sales for private firms, so I construct a proxy for the inventory to sales ratio using the CoreLogic deeds data. I define inventory to sales as the ratio of homes sold in the second half of 2006 built before 2006 to homes sold in 2006.27

The coefficient for the interaction of the shock and the inventory ratio is positive and shows that the effect of the shock increases by 0.28 percentage points when moving from the bottom of the inventory ratio distribution to the top (25th to 75th percentile). Overall, the tendency to cut prices is stronger for firms with higher inventory relative to sales, consistent with firms cutting prices in response to distress when they have more inventory to liquidate.

The first row of the bottom panels ofTable 4reports the effect of the shock calculated at the median value of the financial constraint proxies. The effect of the shock at the median value is positive for every measure used except coverage ratio, indicating that in general, builders were cutting prices in response to losses in other projects in 2009. The results fromTable 3indicate that firms respond to sudden financial distress (the exposure shock) by cutting prices. The results in this section show that the effect of sudden distress on prices is amplified when firms are already financially constrained.

Next, I investigate if the effect of builder losses on prices appears only during periods of costly external finance. During the housing boom, credit standards were loose and builders could easily obtain loans, whereas the opposite was true during the crisis. If the mechanism is financial constraints, then in periods in which builders could easily obtain loans, there should be no tendency to adjust prices following changes in the profits of other projects. To test this implication, I estimate the effect of builderj’s shock measure on prices during the boom years, 2002 to 2005. InEquation (5), I regress the logarithm of the sale price of housei, sold in monthtin 2005, in countyk, by builderj, on builderj’s shock measure, which is calculated excluding house prices in countyk.

β β β γ ε log ( Sale Pric e i , t , j , k , 05 ) = β 1 Δ H P ¯ j , 02 − 05 + β 2 H P t , k + β 3 X i + γ s ( i ) + ε i , t , j , k (5)

Column 4 of panel B ofTable 4presents results fromEquation (5). The sample size is more than three times the size of the sample inTable 3because many more new homes are sold in 2005 than in 2009. The effect in column 4 of panel B is insignificant and close to zero. In specifications with additional housing controls the effect is significant, but importantly, the effect is close to zero (approximately one-fifth to one-tenth the magnitude of the effect inTable 3).29These results indicate that builders react to negative price shocks during the crisis, but not to positive price shocks during the boom, consistent with a financial constraints mechanism. One threat to the design is the possibility that builders learn from house price busts in crisis areas and use this information to mark down prices in healthy regions in anticipation of the crisis spreading. I find no evidence of linkages between projects during the boom period and since learning should occur during both booms and busts, a learning story cannot explain the results.

5.3 Effect on listing times

I find that builders respond to a loss in one project by cutting prices in unaffected projects. For this result to imply that builders cut prices as a result of financial constraints, then it should be the case that when builders cut prices they sell homes faster; that is, builders only cut prices in order to speed up sales and so generate cash flow quickly. In this section, I investigate whether builders experiencing negative shocks are more likely not only to cut prices but also to sell their homes more quickly.30

I regress home listing times on the builder’s shock measure, calculated as inEquation (1), using the same set of controls as inEquation (2). The coefficients reported in the second panel ofTable 5show the change in time on the market in days for a 10% decline in house prices in a builder’s other projects. As I add controls to the specification using the full sample for square footage, bathrooms, and property type, the coefficient remains stable in significance and magnitude. The coefficients in columns 6 and 7 ofTable 5, for time-to-sale and prices, respectively, for public builders, are not significant, implying that for public builders, time-to-sale and prices do not change in response to negative shocks. Together, the results from columns 4 and 5 indicate that builders trade off 2.1% in sale price for selling 14 days sooner, implying an annualized discount rate of 72%. For comparison, the average interest rate on builder debt is 7% during the boom period.34Builders issued no debt during the crisis. Builders likely only use internal financing from accelerated home sales when external financing is excessively costly. If builders relied on an internal discount rate of 72% in 2009, then the rate of external financing must have been greater than 72%, indicating that the cost of external financing between the boom and the bust at the very least grew tenfold in size.

5.4 Heterogeneity by time-to-sale sensitivity

The housing market features search frictions, which produce a positive relationship between price and time-to-sale. If builders are financially constrained and need cash quickly, they should cut prices more in areas where this relationship is stronger, where a given price cut generates a larger decline in time-to-sale. I test this implication in this section by exploring whether builders are more likely to spread negative shocks to projects in areas where time-to-sale responds more strongly to price cuts. For this analysis I use the MLS listings data; details on the sample construction are described inInternet Appendix Section C.5.

To estimate the relationship between time-to-sale and price, ideally I would observe a home’s “true,” quality-adjusted price and then estimate how time-to-sale varies with deviations from the true value (Guren 2018). For example, if a home is overvalued it will take longer to sell. In practice, we do not observe a home’s true value; instead I approximate true value using a hedonic regression. To capture how time-to-sale changes with price, I then estimate the relationship between the probability of sale within a given time and the difference between realized price and predicted price (the markup).

To generate homei’s predicted price,p^i,t⁠, I regress the logarithm of sale price,pi,t⁠, on physical characteristics,Xi, and datetand countykfixed effects,ξξk,t⁠, inEquation (6).

I formalize the relationship between time-to-sale and price followingGuren (2018), modeling the probability of a home selling within 13 weeks,di,t⁠, as a function of predicted markup and date and county fixed effects inEquation (7).

The markup is the residual from the hedonic regression inEquation (6). I estimateEquation (7)grouping markup values into bins, and I plot the coefficients for each markup bin inFigure 4. Consistent withGuren (2018), the relationship between probability of sale and markup inFigure 4is concave and for positive markups the relationship is negative. When homes are overvalued, their probability of sale declines.

To identify regions where price cuts produce larger declines in time-to-sale, I generate sensitivities of time-to-sale for each county.35Next, I divide the main estimation sample into quantiles according to the county time-to-sale elasticity to price and estimateEquation (2)within each quantile. I plot the coefficient for the builder shock measure from the regression within each time-to-sale sensitivity quantile inFigure 5.

5.5 Additional heterogeneity

5.5.1 Reputational concerns

Next, I explore whether the effect of financial constraints on prices varies with reputational concerns. If builders plan to eventually sell many more homes in a development, then slashing prices today to generate cash could hurt them in the future because their future homes will be priced off the discounted sales they make today. This reasoning suggests that comparable pricing or reputational concerns should reduce the extent to which financial constraints drive product pricing. In industries with very large reputational concerns, then financial constraints may not drive firms to slash prices at all.36

To investigate whether firms cut prices less when they face greater reputational concerns, I run several tests. First, I test whether builders that have larger market shares in a county, who should therefore care more about comparable pricing or reputation, tend to cut prices less in response to financial constraints. I define a builder’s county-level market share as the share of homes sales made in 2006 by a builder in a given county. I then interact builder county market share with the shock value in column 7 ofInternet Appendix Table C.13. I find that builders with more market share are less likely to cut prices in response to losses in other projects. Interpreting the result from column 7 ofInternet Appendix Table C.13, going from the 25th percentile of builder county concentration to the 75th percentile, the effect of the shock on prices falls by 50%. This finding suggests that firms are less likely to cut prices when in distress if they have more significant reputational concerns.37

Next, I investigate whether the shock varies with housing attributes, which could correlate with reputational concerns. I test whether builders are more likely to spread shocks to larger, higher-quality homes in columns 1–6 ofInternet Appendix Table C.13. Cutting prices on luxury homes may harm builder reputation less because these homes are more unique and more difficult to benchmark to other homes. In addition, these homes may also have higher profit margins allowing for more flexibility in price. I construct bins of the sample for having above or below median square footage and above or below median number of baths. I also create a variable for “high quality” using the quality indicator code in CoreLogic, equal to one if quality indicator code is “excellent” or “good.” I then interact these variables with the shock measure to test whether builders are more likely to spread shocks to homes that are higher quality.

Consistent with reputational concerns mitigating the effect of financial constraints on prices,Internet Appendix Table C.13indicates that builders are less likely to cut prices on smaller, lower-quality homes (which should be more subject to comparable pricing). Indeed, the coefficient for the builder shock falls by nearly half for homes with below median square footage, and the coefficient for the shock doubles for homes with above median number of baths. The coefficient for the shock is five times as large for high-quality as opposed to low-quality homes. The results on builder concentration and housing attributes together suggest that reputational concerns reduce the tendency for firms to cut prices in response to financial distress.

5.5.2 Builder lending

Next, I explore heterogeneity by home builder mortgage lending. In addition to building homes, large home builders offer mortgages to their home buyers. They bundle the home and mortgage together (Stroebel 2016;Agarwal et al. 2014). I show inInternet Appendix Section C.7that when builders with mortgage lending arms become financially constrained, they offer fewer mortgages. This finding raises the possibility that prices fall for distressed builders not because they cut prices to boost sales, but instead because builders reduce lending, thereby reducing the demand for homes. This channel is consistent with the literature showing that credit supply drives house prices (Adelino, Schoar, and Severino 2012;Justiniano, Primiceri, and Tambalotti 2019;Di Maggio and Kermani 2017).

InInternet Appendix Section C.7I test whether a contraction in mortgage lending from distressed builders can explain the price decline. In columns 1 and 2 ofInternet Appendix Table C.5, I report estimates from a regression of prices on the builder shock measure interacted with an indicator for whether the builder offers mortgages.38If prices fall for distressed builders because builders curtail lending, then the interaction of the lending indicator and the shock will absorb most of the effect of the shock on prices. The interaction of the lending indicator with the shock is insignificant in columns 1 and 2, implying that a reduction in lending from distressed builders does not explain these builders’ lower prices.

Next, I split the sample by whether the builder offers mortgages in columns 3 and 4 and estimateEquation (2)separately in each sample. I repeat this exercise in columns 5 and 6 controlling for additional housing characteristics. If prices fall for distressed builders because they curtail lending, then the effect of the shock will be substantially larger in the sample of builders with lending arms. The effect of the shock is significant and large in the samples of both lending and nonlending builders. The effect shrinks slightly in both samples when additional housing controls are added. I estimate at-test for whether the coefficient for the shock is significantly larger in the lender sample and report thep-values of thet-test in the bottom ofInternet Appendix Table C.5. Across both specifications, I cannot reject the null hypothesis—that the coefficients in the two samples are the same—at the 10% significance level.

Prices do not fall for distressed builders because they reduce lending. Instead, prices fall because distressed builders slash prices to boost sales. The reduction in builder lending may not have mattered for prices, possibly because of the sufficient opportunity for buyers to substitute to other lenders. The lack of a credit channel effect is also consistent with recent empirical work showing a generally weak response of housing demand to credit supply (Fuster and Zafar 2021).
article.6 Additional Robustness:
This section explores a number of robustness checks of the main results inTable 3.

First, the validity of my analysis rests on the assumption that shocked and unshocked builders do not sell homes to different clienteles who respond differently to a common regional price shock. If builders did sell to different socioeconomic groups, then there would be a difference in the luxury or quality of the homes builders sell. To test the possibility that builders sell to different socioeconomic groups, I add a host of additional controls for housing quality toEquation (2), such as roof type, fireplace type, and condition type. I do not include them in my main results inTable 3, because these variables are missing for a large number of observations. Column 2 ofTable 6reports the results. Despite using a much smaller sample, the results remain similar when adding controls for roof type, fireplace type, and condition type.

To allow the effect of quality on price to vary by ZIP code, I add interactions of ZIP code fixed effects and home quality variables to the main specification in column 3 ofTable 6. I find the results remain robust using these even more granular controls, indicating that differences in home quality between builders, which could correlate with differences in builder clientele, do not drive the main results.

Next, I explore how comparable pricing affects the tendency to cut prices in response to distress. Comparable pricing is an important feature of the housing market. Comparable pricing refers to a home’s sale price depending on the past sales of nearby, similar homes.Campbell, Giglio, and Pathak (2011)show evidence for comparable pricing: homes selling in foreclosure tend to decrease the sale prices of nearby homes.39Comparable pricing likely attenuates the effect I find. My identification strategy compares sale prices of homes sold within a narrow geography and time frame, so as to match shocked and unshocked builders as closely as possible. At the same time, homes sold within the same narrow geography and time frame are also more likely to be priced off of each other. A shocked builder slashing prices on his homes in one ZIP code will cause other builder homes in that same ZIP code to decline in price. Comparable pricing of homes should therefore attenuate the effect.

To test that my effect would be stronger in the absence of comparable pricing, I narrow the time fixed effects inEquation (2)from 1 year to 1 month. Comparable pricing is less likely to elicit an effect in more narrow time frames because information on prices takes time to disperse. In the extreme, if two homes sell on the same day, then neither home sale should contaminate the price of the other.

The fourth column ofTable 6reports results from adding ZIP code by year-month fixed effects toEquation (2). The coefficient forβ1is larger than in the specification using only ZIP code by year fixed effects. This result suggests that comparable pricing likely has attenuated the effect inTable 3.

A key threat to my design is the possibility that builders serve different clienteles, and that these clienteles respond differently to common regional house price shocks. I addressed this problem in the previous sections by including controls for home quality and by comparing homes sold within a narrow geography and time frame. Here, I address this problem by estimating a specification that makes it less likely that a builder’s shock is common to all regions. Specifically, I calculate a builder’s shock using only geographically distant counties. I define a builderj’s countyk-specific shock excluding house price changes occurring in counties in the same state as countyk. In column 5 ofTable 6, I report results fromEquation (2), in which builder exposure to other markets has been modified to mean exposure only to markets in other states.

This analysis will exclude builders operating in multiple counties within a single state, and therefore the sample size falls. Nonetheless, the results remain similar when using this more geographically distinct definition of a builder’s exposure to other regions. Interpreting the coefficient from column 5, I find that a builder exposed to a 10% decline in house prices in geographically distant markets cuts prices by 2.1%.

Finally, note that if shocked builders sell to different socioeconomic groups than nonshocked builders, then there should be a difference in the quality of the homes these builders sell. In column 2 ofTable 6, I test this possibility by controlling for a host of home quality characteristics, such as roof type and foundation type, and find the results remain unchanged. Another way to test whether shocked builder homes have lower quality involves analyzing resales of builder homes. The builder’s original financial condition should not affect the price of the home in the future. Therefore, if a distressed builder home’s price is discounted due to the financial condition of the builder, and not due to fundamentals of the home or its clientele, then the price of the home will converge with time to the prices of nondistressed builder homes.

article.7 Conclusion:
This paper uses granular data on home builder housing developments to present new evidence on firm financial constraints and product prices. I use the home building industry as a natural laboratory because it allows me to overcome key measurement and endogeneity challenges related to studying financial constraints and prices. First, I use listings and home sale data to measure builder product pricing, quality, and time-to-sale. Second, losses on housing developments in bust areas suddenly tightened builder financial constraints during the crisis. Builder housing developments are geographically distinct, allowing me to cleanly identify the effect of tighter financial constraints on builder prices in nonbust areas. I find that during the housing crisis, home builders who lost money in housing bust markets cut prices on homes they sold in unaffected markets in order to generate sales quickly. Builders exposed to a one standard deviation decline (approximately 10%) in the value of projects in other markets cut prices in unaffected markets by 2.2%.

A variety of cross-sectional results support the financial constraints mechanism. The tendency to cut prices in unaffected projects following a loss in another, distant project is stronger for firms with higher leverage and lower coverage ratios. I find results only during the bust and not during the boom because financial constraints were not binding in the boom period. When builders cut prices they sell homes faster and builders are more likely to spread price shocks to areas in which price cuts lead to larger declines in time-to-sale, consistent with financially constrained builders cutting prices in order to generate cash flow quickly. Finally, I find that builders are less likely to cut prices on lower-quality homes, which are more affected by builder reputation, consistent with reputational concerns weakening the effect of financial constraints on pricing.

This paper contributes to a literature on the effects of financial frictions on economic activity. I shed light on an important channel—product pricing—through which financial market disruptions can spill over into the real economy.
